lessons.title: 1. Variables & Output
lessons.description: Learn how to store values and display them to the screen.

steps.title: Your First Program
steps.content: Every programmer starts by saying hello! In TinyTalk, we use `show()` to display text on the screen. Text (called a "string") goes inside double quotes.
steps.code:
show("Hello, world!")
show("Welcome to TinyTalk!")

steps.title: Storing Values with let
steps.content:
Variables let you store values and reuse them. Use `let` to create a variable. You can store numbers, text, or anything else. Use `const` for values that should never change.

You can print variables with `show()` — just pass them as space-separated arguments (no commas!).
steps.code:
let name = "Alice"
let age = 25
const PI = 3.14

show("Name:" name)
show("Age:" age)
show("Pi is" PI)

steps.title: String Interpolation
steps.content: Instead of passing multiple arguments to `show()`, you can embed variables directly inside a string using curly braces `{variable}`. This is called string interpolation and it makes your output look clean.
steps.code:
let animal = "cat"
let sound = "meow"

show("The {animal} goes {sound}!")

let price = 9.99
show("Total: ${price}")

steps.title: Introduce Yourself
steps.content:
Create two variables: `name` (set to your name or any name) and `hobby` (set to a hobby). Then use `show()` with string interpolation to print:
`My name is ___ and I love ___!`
steps.starterCode:
// Create your variables below


// Use show() with string interpolation to print the message
steps.hint: Use `let name = "YourName"` and `let hobby = "YourHobby"`, then `show("My name is {name} and I love {hobby}!")`

steps.title: Constants and Variables
steps.content:
Create a `const` called `SPEED_OF_LIGHT` set to `299792458`. Create a `let` variable called `distance` set to `1000`. Then show the message: `Light travels 299792458 m/s over 1000 meters`

Use string interpolation!
steps.starterCode:
// Define the constant and variable


// Show the message using string interpolation
steps.hint: Use `const SPEED_OF_LIGHT = 299792458` and `let distance = 1000`, then `show("Light travels {SPEED_OF_LIGHT} m/s over {distance} meters")`

lessons.title: 2. Numbers & Math
lessons.description: Perform calculations, use math functions, and compare values.

steps.title: Basic Arithmetic
steps.content: TinyTalk supports all the standard math operators: `+` (add), `-` (subtract), `*` (multiply), `/` (divide), `%` (remainder), `**` (power), and `//` (floor division, which rounds down to the nearest whole number).
steps.code:
show("Addition:" 10 + 3)
show("Subtraction:" 10 - 3)
show("Multiplication:" 10 * 3)
show("Division:" 10 / 3)
show("Floor division:" 10 // 3)
show("Remainder:" 10 % 3)
show("Power:" 2 ** 8)

steps.title: Math Functions
steps.content: TinyTalk has built-in math functions you can use right away. `abs()` gives the absolute value, `round()` rounds a number, `sqrt()` gives the square root, and `min()` / `max()` find the smallest or largest value.
steps.code:
show("Absolute value:" abs(-42))
show("Rounded:" round(3.7))
show("Square root:" sqrt(144))
show("Minimum:" min(5, 2, 8, 1))
show("Maximum:" max(5, 2, 8, 1))

steps.title: Comparisons and Natural Operators
steps.content: You can compare values with `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`. TinyTalk also has friendly natural operators: `is` (equals), `isnt` (not equals). These return `true` or `false`.
steps.code:
let x = 10
let y = 20

show(x > y)
show(x < y)
show(x is 10)
show(x isnt y)

let temp = 72
show("Is it warm?" temp > 70)

steps.title: Temperature Converter
steps.content:
Convert a temperature from Fahrenheit to Celsius using the formula: `C = (F - 32) * 5 / 9`.

Set `fahrenheit` to `98.6`, calculate `celsius`, and round the result. Then show: `98.6F is ___C`
steps.hint: Use `let celsius = round((fahrenheit - 32) * 5 / 9)` then `show("{fahrenheit}F is {celsius}C")`

steps.title: Pythagorean Theorem
steps.content:
Given a right triangle with sides `a = 3` and `b = 4`, calculate the hypotenuse `c` using the Pythagorean theorem: `c = sqrt(a**2 + b**2)`.

Show the result as: `The hypotenuse is ___`
steps.hint: Use `let c = sqrt(a**2 + b**2)` — the answer should be 5.

lessons.title: 3. Lists & Maps
lessons.description: Work with collections of data using lists and maps.

steps.title: Creating Lists
steps.content: A list holds multiple values in order. Create one with square brackets `[]`. You can access items by index (starting at 0), check the length with `.len` or `len()`, and add items with `append()`.
steps.code:
let fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

show("First fruit:" fruits[0])
show("Last fruit:" fruits[2])
show("How many?" fruits.len)

append(fruits, "date")
show("After append:" fruits)

steps.title: Creating Maps
steps.content: A map (also called a dictionary) stores key-value pairs. Create one with curly braces `{}`. Access values with `map["key"]`. Use `keys()` and `values()` to get all keys or values.
steps.code:
let person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "Portland"}

show("Name:" person["name"])
show("Age:" person["age"])

show("Keys:" keys(person))
show("Values:" values(person))

steps.title: Lists of Numbers
steps.content: Lists of numbers are great for math. You can use `sum()`, `min()`, `max()`, and `len()` on them.
steps.code:
let scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88]

show("Total:" sum(scores))
show("Best:" max(scores))
show("Worst:" min(scores))
show("Count:" len(scores))
show("Average:" sum(scores) / len(scores))

steps.title: Grocery List
steps.content: Create a list called `groceries` with three items: `"milk"`, `"bread"`, and `"eggs"`. Then append `"butter"` to it. Show the total number of items as: `I need to buy ___ items`
steps.hint: Use `let groceries = ["milk", "bread", "eggs"]`, then `append(groceries, "butter")`, then `show("I need to buy {groceries.len} items")`

steps.title: Student Record
steps.content:
Create a map called `student` with keys `"name"` (set to `"Maya"`), `"grade"` (set to `95`), and `"subject"` (set to `"Math"`). Then show: `Maya scored 95 in Math`

Use string interpolation, accessing the map values with `student["key"]`.
steps.starterCode:
// Create the student map


// Show the formatted message
steps.hint: Create the map with `let student = {"name": "Maya", "grade": 95, "subject": "Math"}` and use `show()` with the values.

lessons.title: 4. Control Flow
lessons.description: Make decisions and repeat actions with if/else, loops, and match.

steps.title: If / Elif / Else
steps.content: Use `if` to run code only when a condition is true. Add `elif` for additional conditions and `else` as a fallback. Conditions use natural operators like `is`, `isnt`, `>`, `<`, etc.

steps.title: For Loops
steps.content: A `for` loop repeats code for each item in a list. The variable after `for` takes on each value one at a time.

steps.title: While Loops and Match
steps.content: `while` loops repeat as long as a condition is true. `match` lets you handle multiple specific values cleanly — like a switch statement.

steps.title: FizzBuzz
steps.content:
Loop through numbers 1 to 5. For each number:
- If divisible by 3, show `Fizz`
- Else, show the number itself

(Hint: use `%` to check divisibility — `n % 3 is 0`)
steps.hint: Use `if n % 3 is 0 { show("Fizz") } else { show(n) }` inside the loop.

steps.title: Biggest Number
steps.content: Given the list `[14, 7, 23, 9, 31, 2]`, use a `for` loop to find the biggest number. Start with a variable `biggest` set to `0`, and update it each time you find something larger. At the end, show: `The biggest number is 31`
steps.starterCode:
let numbers = [14, 7, 23, 9, 31, 2]
let biggest = 0

// Loop through and find the biggest


show("The biggest number is {biggest}")
steps.hint: Inside the loop: `if n > biggest { biggest = n }`

lessons.title: 5. Functions
lessons.description: Define reusable blocks of code with functions.

steps.title: Defining Functions
steps.content: Functions let you name a block of code and reuse it. Define one with `fn`, give it a name and parameters, and use `return` to send back a value.

steps.title: Functions with Logic
steps.content: Functions can contain any logic — conditions, loops, calculations. They help you avoid repeating code and make your programs easier to read.

steps.title: Recursion
steps.content: A function can call itself — this is called recursion. It is great for problems that break into smaller versions of themselves. Always include a base case to stop the recursion!

steps.title: Area Calculator
steps.content: Write a function called `area` that takes `width` and `height` and returns their product (the area of a rectangle). Then call it with `5` and `3`, and show: `Area: 15`
steps.hint: Define `fn area(width, height) { return width * height }`

steps.title: Is It Even?
steps.content:
Write a function `is_even(n)` that returns `true` if `n` is divisible by 2, and `false` otherwise. Then test it by showing the results for 4 and 7:
```
4 is even: true
7 is even: false
```
steps.hint: Use `n % 2 is 0` to check if a number is even. Return `true` or `false`.

lessons.title: 6. Step Chains Basics
lessons.description: Transform data elegantly with TinyTalk's step chain operators.

steps.title: What Are Step Chains?
steps.content: Step chains are TinyTalk's way of transforming data by piping it through a series of operations. Each step starts with an underscore `_` and operates on the result of the previous step. Think of it like an assembly line where data flows through each station.

steps.title: Filter and Map
steps.content: `_filter` keeps only items that match a condition. `_map` transforms each item. Both use lambda syntax: `(x) => expression`.

steps.title: Chaining Steps Together
steps.content: The real power of step chains is combining multiple steps. Data flows left to right through each operation, making complex transformations readable.

steps.title: High Scores
steps.content:
Given the list of scores, use step chains to:
1. Filter to only scores above 80
2. Sort them
3. Reverse (so highest is first)

Show the result. It should be `[95, 92, 88, 85]`.
steps.starterCode:
let scores = [75, 92, 88, 65, 95, 70, 85, 55]

// Use step chains to filter, sort, and reverse
let top_scores = scores
show(top_scores)
steps.hint: Chain them: `scores _filter((s) => s > 80) _sort _reverse`

steps.title: Transform and Take
steps.content:
Given a list of numbers, use step chains to:
1. Map each number to its square (`n ** 2`)
2. Filter to keep only squares greater than 10
3. Sort the result
4. Take the first 3

Show the result.
steps.starterCode:
let nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

// Square, filter > 10, sort, take 3
let result = nums
show(result)
steps.hint: Use `nums _map((n) => n ** 2) _filter((n) => n > 10) _sort _take(3)`

lessons.title: 7. Step Chains Advanced
lessons.description: Master advanced step chain operations for powerful data transformations.

steps.title: Unique and Flatten
steps.content: `_unique` removes duplicate values from a list. `_flatten` takes a list of lists and merges them into a single flat list.
steps.code:
let tags = ["js", "python", "js", "rust", "python", "go", "rust"]
show("Unique tags:" tags _unique)

let nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]
show("Flattened:" nested _flatten)

// Combine: flatten then get unique
let data = [["a", "b"], ["b", "c"], ["a", "d"]]
show("Flat + unique:" data _flatten _unique)

steps.title: Reduce
steps.content: `_reduce` combines all items in a list into a single value. It takes a lambda with two parameters: the accumulator and the current item. You can optionally provide a starting value.

steps.title: Complex Chains
steps.content: You can build sophisticated data pipelines by combining many step chain operations. Each step transforms the data and passes it along.

steps.title: Word Processor
steps.content:
Given a list of words (some duplicated), use step chains to:
1. Get only unique words
2. Filter to words longer than 3 characters (use `.len`)
3. Sort them alphabetically

Show the result.
steps.starterCode:
let words = ["the", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "comfortable", "mat", "the", "cat", "purred", "on", "it"]

// Get unique, filter long words, sort
let result = words
show(result)
steps.hint: Use `words _unique _filter((w) => w.len > 3) _sort`

steps.title: Running Total
steps.content: Use `_reduce` to calculate the product of all numbers in the list `[2, 3, 4, 5]`. Show the result as: `Product: 120`
steps.hint: Use `nums _reduce((acc, n) => acc * n)` and show the result.

lessons.title: 8. Data Analysis
lessons.description: Work with structured data using list-of-maps and powerful step chains.

steps.title: Lists of Maps as Data Tables
steps.content: In TinyTalk, structured data is represented as lists of maps — like rows in a spreadsheet. Each map is a record, and each key is a column. Step chains become incredibly powerful on this kind of data.
steps.code:
let students = [
  {"name": "Alice", "grade": 92, "subject": "Math"},
  {"name": "Bob", "grade": 85, "subject": "English"},
  {"name": "Carol", "grade": 95, "subject": "Math"},
  {"name": "Dave", "grade": 78, "subject": "English"},
  {"name": "Eve", "grade": 88, "subject": "Math"}
]

show("First student:" students[0])
show("First name:" students[0]["name"])
show("Total students:" len(students))

steps.title: Filtering and Selecting Data
steps.content: `_filter` works on data rows just like on simple lists. Use `_select` to pick specific columns, and `_arrange` to sort by a particular field.

steps.title: Statistics on Data
steps.content: TinyTalk has built-in statistics functions that work great with numeric data extracted from your records. Use `mean()`, `median()`, `sd()`, and `summary()` to quickly analyze numbers.
steps.code:
let temps = [72, 75, 68, 80, 77, 73, 69, 85, 71, 74]

show("Mean:" mean(temps))
show("Median:" median(temps))
show("Std dev:" round(sd(temps)))
show("Summary:" summary(temps))

steps.title: Filter the Data
steps.content: Given the list of products, use step chains to find all products in the `"Electronics"` category with a price greater than 50. Show the filtered results.
steps.starterCode:
let products = [
  {"name": "Laptop", "category": "Electronics", "price": 999},
  {"name": "Pen", "category": "Office", "price": 2},
  {"name": "Headphones", "category": "Electronics", "price": 79},
  {"name": "Notebook", "category": "Office", "price": 5},
  {"name": "Mouse", "category": "Electronics", "price": 25},
  {"name": "Monitor", "category": "Electronics", "price": 299}
]

// Filter to Electronics over $50
let result = products
show(result)
steps.hint: Chain two filters: `products _filter((p) => p["category"] is "Electronics") _filter((p) => p["price"] > 50)`

steps.title: Analyze Scores
steps.content:
Given the list of test scores, calculate and display the mean and median. Show them as:
```
Mean: (value)
Median: (value)
```
steps.starterCode:
let scores = [88, 92, 75, 95, 83, 79, 91, 86, 70, 97]

// Calculate and show the mean and median
steps.hint: Use `show("Mean:" mean(scores))` and `show("Median:" median(scores))`.

lessons.title: 9. Charts & Visualization
lessons.description: Create beautiful charts and visualizations from your data.

steps.title: Bar Charts
steps.content: Use `chart_bar()` to create bar charts. Pass in a list of labels, a list of values, and an optional title. Bar charts are perfect for comparing categories.
steps.code:
let languages = ["Python", "JavaScript", "Rust", "Go", "TinyTalk"]
let popularity = [30, 25, 15, 12, 18]

chart_bar(languages, popularity, "Programming Language Popularity")

steps.title: Line and Scatter Charts
steps.content: `chart_line()` shows trends over time or sequences. `chart_scatter()` shows relationships between two variables. Both take x values, y values, and a title.
steps.code:
// Line chart: temperature over a week
let days = ["Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"]
let temps = [72, 75, 68, 80, 77, 82, 79]
chart_line(days, temps, "Weekly Temperature")

// Scatter plot: study hours vs test scores
let hours = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]
let scores = [55, 60, 65, 72, 78, 85, 90, 95]
chart_scatter(hours, scores, "Study Hours vs Test Scores")

steps.title: Pie Charts and Histograms
steps.content: `chart_pie()` shows proportions of a whole. `chart_histogram()` shows the distribution of a dataset by grouping values into bins.
steps.code:
// Pie chart: budget breakdown
let categories = ["Rent", "Food", "Transport", "Fun", "Savings"]
let amounts = [1200, 400, 200, 150, 300]
chart_pie(categories, amounts, "Monthly Budget")

// Histogram: exam score distribution
let exam_scores = [65, 72, 78, 80, 82, 85, 85, 88, 90, 91, 93, 95, 97, 100]
chart_histogram(exam_scores, 5, "Exam Score Distribution")

steps.title: Visualize Fruit Sales
steps.content: Create a bar chart showing fruit sales. Use these labels: `["Apples", "Bananas", "Oranges", "Grapes"]` and these values: `[150, 230, 180, 95]`. Give it the title `"Fruit Sales"`.
steps.starterCode:
// Create the labels and values lists


// Create a bar chart
steps.hint: Use `chart_bar(["Apples", "Bananas", "Oranges", "Grapes"], [150, 230, 180, 95], "Fruit Sales")`

steps.title: Score Distribution
steps.content: Given the list of test scores, create a histogram with 4 bins and title `"Test Score Distribution"`. Then separately show the mean score.
steps.hint: Use `chart_histogram(scores, 4, "Test Score Distribution")` and `show("Mean:" mean(scores))`.

lessons.title: 10. Blueprints & OOP
lessons.description: Create your own types with blueprints, fields, and methods.

steps.title: What Are Blueprints?
steps.content: Blueprints are TinyTalk's version of classes. They let you define your own types with `field` for data and `law` for methods. You create instances using `forge`. Think of a blueprint as a cookie cutter — you define the shape once, then stamp out as many cookies as you want.

steps.title: Methods with Logic
steps.content: Methods (called `law` in TinyTalk) can contain any logic — conditions, loops, calculations. Use `self` to access the object's own fields.

steps.title: Methods That Return Values
steps.content: Methods can return values just like functions. This lets you use blueprints in calculations and chain operations together.

steps.title: Build a Pet
steps.content:
Create a blueprint called `Pet` with fields `name` and `species`, and a method `introduce()` that shows: `Hi, I'm ___ the ___!`

Then forge a pet and call `introduce()` on it.
steps.starterCode:
// Define the Pet blueprint


// Forge a pet and introduce it
steps.hint: Use `blueprint Pet { field name; field species; law introduce() { show("Hi, I'm {self.name} the {self.species}!") } }` then `let p = forge Pet("Buddy", "dog")` and `p.introduce()`.

steps.title: Counter Blueprint
steps.content:
Create a `Counter` blueprint with a field `count` (starting value will be passed in). Add three methods:
- `increment()` — adds 1 to count and shows the new count
- `decrement()` — subtracts 1 from count and shows the new count
- `value()` — returns the current count

Forge a counter starting at 0, increment it 3 times, decrement once, then show the final value using `show("Final:" c.value())`.

Expected output should end with `Final: 2`.
steps.hint: Define `law increment() { self.count = self.count + 1; show(self.count) }` and similar for decrement. Use `law value() { return self.count }`.
